items.headline: Comm Hill Phase 3.1 housing development approved on Altino Boulevard
items.summary: Four new homes permitted as part of Phase 3.1 of the Comm Hill development along Altino Boulevard, ranging from $487K-$573K. This represents a significant residential expansion in the area with multiple unit types and continued buildout of a larger master-planned community.

items.headline: Sherly Place residential project underway with three new homes
items.summary: Three new homes approved at Sherly Place, a small infill residential project with units priced $496K-$570K. Represents targeted neighborhood development in San José's ongoing housing construction.

items.headline: City Council reviews FY 2026-27 capital improvement projects
items.summary: Mountain View City Council held a study session on the capital improvement program for fiscal year 2026-27, which outlines planned infrastructure and facility upgrades. This is worth tracking if you care about what projects—like roads, parks, or public facilities—the city plans to invest in over the next year.

items.headline: Sunnyvale considers changes to tenant protection rules
items.summary: The City Council is introducing an ordinance to amend the Residential Tenant Protections Program. This could affect renters' rights and protections in Sunnyvale, making it worth monitoring for potential impacts on local housing stability.

items.headline: City releases Children and Youth Services Master Plan status update
items.summary: San José reported progress on its comprehensive plan for youth services, including programs like the Safe Summer Initiative and Youth Intervention Services. This signals how the city is investing in young people across the South Bay.

items.headline: Library Facilities and Customer Experience Plan moves forward
items.summary: The city provided a status report on plans to improve San José library facilities and services. This affects where residents can access library programs and what those experiences will look like going forward.

items.headline: Downtown building seeks historic status upgrade and 5,000 sq ft bonus
items.summary: The owner of 405 Kipling Street is requesting to elevate the building's historic designation from Category 4 to Category 2, which would allow an additional 5,000 square feet of floor area as an incentive for seismic and historic rehabilitation. This could affect the building's future size and use in downtown Palo Alto.
